title: India Discovery Hub
subtitle: Reimagining information access for diverse Indian web users, from scratch.
problem_statement: The current web interface, as visually presented, offers a minimalist global search experience centered on a single text input field and two generic search buttons ("Google Search", "I'm Feeling Lucky"). While it includes language options ("Google offered in: हिन्दी বাংলা తెలుగు मराठी தமிழ் ગુજરાતી ಕನ್ನಡ മലയാളം ਪੰਜਾਬੀ") and a regional event banner ("Ready, set, I/O. Tune in for our latest AI announcements tomorrow at 10:30pm IST"), it lacks immediate, visually prominent pathways or curated content tailored for the diverse, hyper-local information and service discovery needs of the Indian market, suggesting a significant market gap for a zero-to-one platform.

pillars.title: Contextual Relevance
pillars.insight: The current UI's primary visual element is a search bar, implying users know exactly what to search for. For a new platform, a gap exists in proactively offering contextually relevant local information (e.g., local news, events, services) without explicit user input, especially in a diverse market like India.

pillars.title: Accessibility & Inclusivity
pillars.insight: While language options are present in the footer, the core interaction remains text-based search. A zero-to-one platform could innovate by offering more diverse input methods (e.g., visual search, voice search with regional accent recognition beyond the microphone icon) and content presentation formats to cater to varying digital literacy levels and preferences across India.

pillars.title: Efficiency of Discovery
pillars.insight: The current UI requires users to formulate and type queries. For common, recurring local needs (e.g., finding nearby shops, checking local transport), this can be inefficient. A new platform could offer direct, visually accessible shortcuts or curated feeds to streamline discovery for everyday Indian use cases.

artifacts.label: Strategy
artifacts.title: Defining the Challenges
questions: How might we build a zero-to-one information discovery platform that deeply integrates local Indian context, services, and content beyond a generic search bar, to capture market share?
How might we enable Indian web users to discover relevant local information and services more intuitively and directly, reducing reliance on complex text queries on a blank page?

How might we leverage visual elements, regional language content, and community features to make information access more culturally resonant and efficient for diverse Indian users, starting from scratch?

personas.name: Rohan Sharma
personas.quote: I need quick, reliable info about local market trends and suppliers, not just global news.
personas.bio: Rohan runs a small electronics shop. He uses the internet primarily for business research and staying updated on local regulations. He finds generic search results often too broad and struggles to find specific local business tools or information in Marathi.
needs: Direct access to government business portals
frustrations: Lack of specific local business tools

personas.name: Priya Singh
personas.quote: I want to easily find local events, study groups, and authentic regional recipes without typing long queries.
personas.bio: Priya is pursuing her Masters. She's tech-savvy but often struggles to find specific local cultural events or community information relevant to Lucknow through generic search. She prefers visual browsing and quick access to information.
needs: Easy access to local community forums
frustrations: Lack of visual browsing options for local interests

insights: "Where can I find a good local tailor?",
"Is there a festival happening nearby this week?",
"This search result is too general, I need something specific to my city.",
"I wish there was a quick way to see what's trending in my area.",
"This blank search bar feels a bit intimidating for complex local queries.",
"Am I typing the right keywords to get local results?",
"There must be a better way to discover local services than just typing.",
"The 'I/O announcements' isn't relevant to my daily needs.",
Types specific, often long, queries to narrow down local results.
Clicks through multiple links to find relevant local information.
Uses other apps (e.g., social media) for local discovery.
Overwhelmed by the sheer volume of information.
Relieved when a local result is finally found.
Disconnected from the platform's ability to understand local context.

points.action: Substitute the blank background with dynamic, location-aware content cards displaying local news headlines, trending topics in India, or popular local services, instead of just a search bar.
points.benefit: Provides immediate value and context, reducing the cognitive load of starting from a blank slate.

points.action: Combine the search bar functionality with direct, visually prominent access to common Indian services (e.g., local transport, food delivery, government portals) via quick-access buttons or a personalized dashboard.
points.benefit: Streamlines access to frequently needed local services, making the platform a one-stop hub.

points.action: Adapt the 'I'm Feeling Lucky' concept to 'I'm Feeling Local' or 'Explore My City,' offering curated, hyper-local discovery experiences

stages.touchpoints: Blank search bar on the web page
stages.actions: User sees the prominent search bar and needs to find a local service (e.g., 'best street food in Delhi').
stages.friction: Requires precise query formulation; no immediate visual cues for local discovery.

stages.actions: User types 'best street food Delhi' and clicks 'Google Search'.
stages.friction: The page offers no proactive suggestions for local context or popular local searches before typing.

stages.actions: User scans results, looking for local blogs, reviews, or directories.
stages.friction: Results might be generic, not hyper-local, or require further filtering. The initial page doesn't prepare for this.

stages.touchpoints: Search bar again, or navigates away
stages.actions: User adds more specific keywords or opens a different app (e.g., Zomato, local news app).
stages.friction: The initial page doesn't offer alternative discovery paths (e.g., 'Explore Delhi Food' button).

stages.touchpoints: External app, or deep within search results
stages.actions: User eventually finds a local food blog or a specific restaurant listing.
stages.friction: The initial web page didn't facilitate this discovery directly or efficiently for a local need.
